title: P-Wave Velocity Test (Ultrasonic Pulse Velocity)
aim: To determine the P-wave velocity of the given rock specimen.
theory: P-waves (Primary waves) are compressional waves that travel through solids, liquids, and gases.
In rocks, P-wave velocity depends on density, elasticity, and porosity.
Higher velocity indicates stronger, denser, and less fractured rock.
Lower velocity indicates weak, porous, or cracked rock.
The test is based on measuring the travel time of ultrasonic pulses through the specimen.
A transmitter generates ultrasonic waves which pass through the rock.
A receiver detects the waves after traveling through the specimen.
Velocity is calculated as the ratio of length to travel time.
V = L / T, where V is velocity, L is length, and T is time taken.
It is a non-destructive test widely used in rock mechanics.
It helps in evaluating rock quality, cracks, and internal defects.
Used in geotechnical, mining, and construction engineering.
procedure: The rock specimen is prepared with smooth and parallel faces.
The length of the specimen is measured accurately using a scale or vernier caliper.
The surfaces of the specimen are cleaned properly.
Coupling gel is applied on both ends of the specimen to ensure proper transmission of waves.
The transmitter and receiver transducers are placed on opposite faces of the specimen.
The UPV apparatus is switched on and properly calibrated.
Ultrasonic pulse is generated and allowed to pass through the specimen.
The travel time of the pulse is displayed on the device.
Multiple readings are taken for accuracy.
The average travel time is calculated.
P-wave velocity is calculated using V = L / T.
The result is recorded and analyzed.
result: The P-wave velocity of the given rock sample is found to be 4 km/s and 5 km/s indicating good to excellent rock quality.
